info.parenttitle: C4.1 Design for manufacture strategies (HL only)

markdown:
## Design for Manufacture (DfM)


<definition term="Design for Manufacture (DfM)">A **design approach** where products are created for **optimal use of existing manufacturing capabilities**, improving **efficiency and ease of production**.</definition>

It encompasses three main strategies:

  1. **Design for Process**
  2. **Design for Assembly**
  3. **Design for Disassembly**

### Design for Process


<definition term="Design for Process">**Designing a product specifically to suit a particular manufacturing method**, such as **injection moulding**, to ensure efficient and cost-effective production.</definition>

![A plastic bottle designed with uniform wall thickness to suit blow moulding is an example of design for process (nexams)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1c09f1b8322ef3850b47cc239991ed079d5ee583-1536x1024.png)

### Design for Assembly


<definition term="Design for Assembly">**Designing a product to simplify how parts fit together**, including **joining components**, forming **subassemblies**, and building the **final product** efficiently.</definition>

![IKEA furniture is designed with assembly in mind as it using snap-fit joints instead of screws ](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0071996dd603c286e8278c5633683481216c9f16-259x194.png)

### Design for Disassembly


<definition term="Design for Disassembly">**Designing a product so it can be easily and cost-effectively taken apart**, allowing **components to be reused or repaired** and **materials to be recycled**.</definition>

![The Fairphone is an example of design for disassembly as it has modular components that can be easily replaced.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/39f865fc7ad24465d70889224975dce68c28f7c8-1600x548.png)
